section_title: INTRODUCTION TO MARKET STRUCTURE ESSENTIALS AND TRADE DIRECTION
content:
This module, 'Essentials To Market Structure,' aims to help you educate yourself in determining trade
direction. This is one of the most reoccurring email inquiries and forum posts. My goal is to share further
amplification on some of the concepts I use in determining trade direction. As a Forex trader, you want to
be finding your way through price. For a new trader, this task can be daunting due to the many different
timeframes available: monthly, weekly, daily, four-hour, one-hour, fifteen-minutes, five-minutes, one-minute,
and tick charts. It's bewildering if you don't understand how to break down price in a uniform, structured
way.

section_title: DEFINING YOUR TRADING TIMEFRAME AND STYLE
content:
Your primary objective is to know your timeframe that you're trading. This relates to what type of trader you
are going to be:
- Position trader
- Swing trader
- Short-term trader
- Day trader
- Scalper
I cannot teach you how to find the correct style of trader that's within you; that's part of your personal
makeup. This module will speak in general terms, giving you enough insight to determine what you need to
focus on for your most optimal way of trading. Each of us will be different. As you grow and mature as a
trader, you may become multi-timeframe based.

section_title: RECOMMENDED STARTING STYLES AND PROFESSIONAL TIMEFRAME
content:
PERSPECTIVES
I consider myself a dynamic trader because I can trade any one of these timeframes. I excel in the short
- term to swing trading area, but I can day trade and scalp, though I prefer not to. My advice is to try to work
within the short-term to day trading in the beginning, as it gives the most immediate feedback and
confidence to stick to a plan.
Position traders don't have many opportunities frequently. Swing trading also has longer waits between
setups, making it harder for new traders to stick to the framework. Short-term trading and day trading are
very rewarding for new price action traders due to immediate feedback.
Let's talk about the professional perspective when applying market structure:
- For Position Trades: Duration is between three to six months to a year. Utilize monthly, weekly, and daily
charts. Monthly is the highest, weekly the mid-line, and daily the short-term.

section_title: TIMEFRAME BREAKDOWN FOR DIFFERENT TRADING STYLES
content:
For a Swing Trader:
- Premise for breaking down market structure will be comprised of the daily, four-hour, and one-hour charts.
- The setup is based on the daily chart (highest timeframe for swing trades).
- If the daily shows considerately overbought conditions or a topping formation, you'd look for market
structure breakdown on the weekly and daily to facilitate a short position (similar to monthly for position
trades).
- Timing is on the one-hour chart.
- Management is on the four-hour chart.
- The trade idea is built upon the daily chart.

For a Short-Term Trader:
- Duration is one day to a week or so (swing trading is about a week or more).
- Utilize the four-hour chart for your trade premise or directional bias.
- The one-hour chart is for trade management or as the mid-level timeframe.
- The 15-minute chart is for timing entry and looking for early reversal signs to take profits.
For Day Trades:
- The one-hour chart is your highest timeframe.
- Manage your trade on the 15-minute timeframe.
- The five-minute chart is utilized to enter.
It's not to say you can't use a five-minute chart on short-term, swing, and position trades for entry. This
framework provides three timeframes to break down market structure for each trading model, giving you
the building blocks for directional bias.

section_title: KEYS TO MULTIPLE TIMEFRAME MARKET STRUCTURE
content:
The keys to multiple timeframe market structure are rather simple:
- Your focus should be on the highest of the three timeframes.
- Trades will be managed by the highest or mid-level timeframes.
- For a swing trader, the daily timeframe facilitates the trade premise (directional bias), and the four-hour
chart is used to manage the trade.
- The one-hour chart (shortest timeframe for swing trading) is used for timing purposes.
- Entry signals are derived from studying market structure on the daily and four-hour charts, then the one
- hour chart facilitates the specific entry point.
- The highest probability trades are made in the higher timeframe direction. However, if the higher
timeframe premise is bullish but approaching a key resistance level, that may be trumped.

section_title: THE SUPREMACY OF SUPPORT AND RESISTANCE
content:
Support and resistance trumps everything. Without understanding key support and resistance levels, you
won't get to a directional bias, regardless of your trading model (position, day trading, etc.). If it's not framed
on the premise of key support and resistance levels, it will be a struggling point for you.
- You have to go back to the core essentials of sound trading: key support and resistance levels are where
it's all at.
- Without these, all other procedures and concepts will do no good.
- The highest timeframe for your particular model should draw your attention to relevant key support and
resistance levels. You can always check the daily and weekly charts as a catch-all for support and
resistance.
- Market profiles (trending, reversal, consolidation) also assist in market structure analysis and measuring
current market structure.

section_title: UNDERSTANDING MARKET STRUCTURE SHIFTS FOR BEARISH REVERSALS
content:
When we're looking at market structure, we're asking: are we bullish or bearish? If price rallies up and hits
a major resistance level (perceived as your higher timeframe), price never moves in a straight line. There
will be consolidations and moves up. As price reaches this resistance level, we anticipate a reversal.
- When markets start to break down, taking out short-term swing lows on the highest timeframe, this is the
catalyst.
- This indicates a market structure shift.
- We anticipate these events unfolding; we don't know the future but framework our thinking based on
expected price action once the structure is broken.
- For example, if a monthly chart hits key resistance and takes out a short-term low, market structure has
broken.

section_title: MULTI-TIMEFRAME CONFLUENCE AND NAVIGATING THE 'GRAY AREA'
content:
If we anticipate a resistance level, we expect a retracement for a trade entry. We would then zoom in to a
weekly timeframe to hone in on shorter-term price levels, potentially finding an optimal trade entry or a sell
pattern that confirms the bearish scenario. Further zooming into a daily chart could show similar patterns,
creating nesting confluences of implied resistance levels.
- Once a higher timeframe market structure is broken down, we can position ourselves in sync with a top
- down approach.
- As price trades down, there's a 'gray area' missing gaps where we don't know what will happen
between entry and target. You must be comfortable with this uncertainty.
- The overall framework of price structure involves consolidations and moves. When looking for bullishness,
we want to see support being held and resistance being broken. Every pullback offers opportunities for
optimal trade entries or reflection buys, looking for price finding support and breaking resistance. That's the
whole framework behind market structure.

section_title: DYNAMIC MARKET STRUCTURE AND MARKET MAKER PROFILES
content:
As price rallies into a shorter-term resistance level, it may find support. But if it breaks down, be
comfortable with price blowing out previous lows. An important low may have formed, with trailing stop-loss
orders below it. A dip down offers another opportunity to get long.
- On a higher timeframe premise, when we see consolidation and higher-level resistance (known in
advance through support/resistance studies), if price continues to maintain support and breaks above
short-term highs, market structure implies a potential leg up (e.g., duplicating a prior low-to-high swing).
- Consolidation areas are crucial for studying dynamic support/resistance levels, as they are more easily
tradable due to discernible price levels.
- We don't know how price moves between consolidations, highlighting the 'gray area' (missing pieces of
market structure).
- Apply this diagram's general 'profile' of price lows and declines to market maker profiles. You can see the
same premise. Prices are engineered to go lower into support to trade higher these are our building
blocks.

section_title: ANTICIPATING BULLISH SHIFTS FROM BEARISH STRUCTURE
content:
We must live in the gray area and not expect a simple black and white premise to trading. When we see a
consolidation, price moving down to a new consolidation, and then short-term breaks in market structure (a
market structure shift) as price trades down to a net support level (assuming this is the highest level of your
three timeframes), we would anticipate a bullish move higher.
- However, moving into a new consolidation, this may not always translate into even higher prices. It could
be reaching up just to go back to a previous range (e.g., from an old high to a current low), referencing
'inside the range concepts'.
- If getting long at a low, expect some profit-taking at higher levels, visible on your mid-level chart for swing
projections.
- As price comes down, every retracement offering new optimal trade entries means looking for price
holding support, breaking resistance on the side of support being found. But trading down into support, we
anticipate market structure to break lows, find resistance, break lows, find resistance, etc.
- Every new consolidation, if expecting price to reach a higher-level support on the highest timeframe, is
why we do our analysis on the highest level to see where price is probably reaching for. This gives the
highest probability of success. If price doesn't get to these points and we see early market shifts, it could
be a catalyst for another type of trade (e.g., getting long for a move within a range, offering good reward-to
- risk).

section_title: BEARISH MARKET STRUCTURE AND SWING PROJECTIONS
content:
Assuming a high reaching into resistance, as price breaks a short-term low, market structure is broken. As
price retraces, we look for new selling opportunities.
- If a previous swing's low is taken out, and we get a retracement or additional sell signal, we use swing
projections (not necessarily Fibonacci, but simple price action).
- Take the measurable swing from the previous low to high. Once broken, this same measurable swing can
be projected from the breaking point lower, yielding a target area. I teach to exit the trade before the actual
objective is met.
- Every time a swing is broken (e.g., a low is broken), if price retraces back into that area, it can still set up
an optimal trade entry. I purposely show price moving past the expected resistance to illustrate the
'grayness' of support and resistance; you need flexibility.
- A deeper pullback can still set up an optimal trade entry. Even if not the exact level, it operates within the
previous range, maintaining bearish market structure. Don't be fooled into thinking it's a long to go higher if
it's a higher-level resistance level.
- Every short-term retracement can be a catalyst for an additional entry using our smallest of the three
timeframes, modeling off the highest timeframe. Once a swing low is broken, the mid-level chart can be
used for additional entries or managing existing positions.

section_title: MEASURED LEGS, RETRACEMENTS, AND PRICE SWINGS
content:
When this swing up is broken, it's an engineered swing a measured move. Take the range from the low to
this high, subtract it from that same point, and project it lower. This is a measured swing.
- Similarly, a measured leg move: from a high down to a low. Price retraces, and we see this as an
intermediate-term price swing or price leg.
- We blend two concepts: a broken swing within a bearish market structure, and a retracement back into a
previous broken support (which should act as resistance).
- What looks clear on charts may not be as clear-cut in reality, but we expect an intermediate-term
retracement for a new leg down. The range from the high to low of this leg is replicated and projected from
the new high, looking for a similar move.
- Intermediate-term highs are highs with lower highs on either side. Intermediate-term lows are lows with
two higher lows on either side. These are easy to identify by looking at candles/bars. Note them on your
charts; by nesting them out, you can classify them as intermediate-term or even long-term (if there are
lower highs on either side of a high for a long time) and expect longer-term price swings.

section_title: NESTING PRICE STRUCTURES AND PROFIT PROJECTIONS
content:
The leg discussed earlier can be seen by having an intermediate-term high noted. This is an intermediate
- term price leg, and then an intermediate-term retracement.
- The next leg down would be replicated. The range between the low to high is what you see replicated,
causing you to expect price movement there.
- If you're still in a trade after a retracement, you can expect to hold onto that trade to get back down to
these lows, which would hopefully converge with a higher-level support level to take profits.
- The framework of your market structure is derived from the highest level of the three timeframes you're
trading with; that's the basis of your trade. Your mid-level chart is used to zero down into a smaller
timeframe, expecting to find support/resistance levels not discernible on your highest timeframe. Your
lowest timeframe is used for your entry.

Assuming we've built the premise of market structure and it's bullish (everything would be reversed for
selling scenarios):
- As a swing trader, if our highest timeframes suggest a bullish market structure underway, we've traded off
a higher-level support, and price has given us a broken market structure to the upside (a market structure
shift where short-term highs have been broken).
- Our mid-level timeframe allows us to zero in and find a key support/resistance level. Now, this higher-level
key support/resistance converges with our mid-level support/resistance, perhaps with an overlapping
pattern.
- We now have a bullish bias. This doesn't mean every single day will yield a bullish, profitable trade. If it
were that simple, everyone would be multimillionaires. You need discernment and to allow for some failure.
- Our bias is to buy. This doesn't mean other traders can't make money shorting on that specific day; it just
means you stick to being a bull for that particular day or timeframe.

section_title: TRADING WITH KILL ZONES AND TIME & PRICE THEORY
content:
With your bias in mind, always go back to the key premise of trading within kill zones:
- London Open
- New York Open
- London Close
- Asia
Assuming we've arrived at our trading time of day, understand the kill zone start, have our key support/
resistance identified, and know where price should get to before doing anything (this specific area), this is
our action point. We enter here, either on a limit basis or as a market order, utilizing time and price theory.
- In your mind, you anticipate these events unfolding within a specific time of day with a specific bias. You
want these things to line up.
- The secret is that every day, the bias is both directions. Traders make money going long and short daily.
You have to decide what you're trading based on your timeframe and trader profile (short-term, swing,
position). You look for your bias to line up with price action. You can't force price; you can only get in sync
with what it may be doing and allow yourself a ride.

section_title: THE ACTION PLAN AND PROFESSIONAL TRADING MINDSET
content:
With a bullish premise, if price trades back down to an established respectable support level within a kill
zone, we take action to buy. You wait until the kill zone starts, and when price hits that specific point, you
use your chosen entry technique or concept:
- Optimal Trade Entry (OTE)
- Reflection
- Grail
- Stinger
It must happen at a key support/resistance level, with the highest timeframe giving you the bias. This is
your action plan; you do nothing else. A sell pattern for a very short-term trader, who makes money trading
down, is not your trade.
- Don't try to force more out of the concept than intended. You're simply looking for a bias for your style of
trading. It doesn't mean you'll be right all the time; take that out of the equation. You're just looking to get in
sync with price action based on your premise or style.
- This simple approach helps avoid over-complication, common with new traders who add too many things,
falsely expecting to always know market direction. I've been doing this for almost 20 years and don't get it
right every day. The secret is to wait until everything lines up the majority of my tools, based on my
understanding of market profile (reversal, trending, consolidation).

By selecting a directional bias, this does not guarantee profitability or accuracy in trade direction or results.
One trader's bullish bias and another's sell bias can both be correct and profitable, even achieving their
respective objectives. They can also both be wrong and still make money, despite objectives not being met.
They could also both lose money.
- There is no black and white. As a trader, you must enter the gray and be comfortable with less than
perfect visibility. The foresight you expect doesn't exist; nobody has a crystal ball. I trade on probabilities,
not perfect scenarios.
- You will see trades materialize that provide plenty of profit-taking potential. Don't expect 100%; it's an
impossibility that will drive you nuts.
- Find your timeframe as a trader, determine the market structure for that timeframe, trade within that
respective market structure, and perform your targeting on the highest and mid-level timeframes. This will
give you more than enough trades.
- It's not about trading every day or capturing a thousand pips a month. It's about consistently harvesting
profits, keeping risk low, and your action level low. Control your trading to control your emotions, keep
expectations realistic, and live comfortably in the gray.
